title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: Mount Everest has become a high-altitude playground for the wealthy, a troubling trend that is as dangerous as it is disrespectful to the mountain and to the communities who regard it as sacred. [37]The presence of inexperienced 'tourists' who lack even the most basic mountaineering skills forces Sherpas to take extreme and often fatal risks to ensure their clients' survival.[/37] This is no longer genuine exploration; it is a luxury commodity, packaged and sold to those with deep pockets but dangerously shallow experience. [38]The environmental toll—tons of abandoned oxygen canisters and accumulated human waste—is a stark testament to the complete lack of reverence shown for these sacred peaks.[/38] The ecological and cultural integrity of the high mountains is being sacrificed daily for the fleeting vanity of the privileged. [40]We must strictly limit permits to those who can demonstrate a genuine lifetime of serious technical climbing experience.[/40] No permit fee should be sufficient to purchase access to these fragile environments.
texts.B: The widespread indignation over the so-called 'crowding' of Everest too often reeks of elitism carefully dressed up as environmental concern. Why should the world's highest peaks be the exclusive domain of a self-appointed climbing elite who conveniently forget they were once beginners themselves? [39]The commercialization of these peaks has brought vital modern infrastructure and much-needed income to some of the most impoverished mountain communities on earth.[/39] While the environmental concerns are entirely valid and deserve serious attention, the appropriate solution is better, more rigorous management of waste, logistics, and visitor conduct—not the wholesale exclusion of people who have dreamed their entire lives of standing on the roof of the world. [37]Adventure should be accessible to anyone with the financial means, the determination, and the genuine will to train adequately for the challenge.[/37] Gatekeeping these mountains serves no higher purpose except to preserve the privileges of those who already stand at the top.
texts.C: The real and most lasting tragedy of commercialized high-altitude climbing is the fundamental shift in the nature of personal responsibility that has quietly taken place over the past two decades. In traditional alpinism, each climber accepted complete personal responsibility for their own life and every decision made on the mountain; in the modern guided commercial model, however, the professional guide is effectively expected to serve as a virtual guarantor of client safety. This creates a dangerous psychological 'safety net' that actively encourages reckless behavior in the extremely thin air where cognitive judgment is already significantly impaired by altitude. [39]I agree that the local mountain economy benefits substantially from this industry[/39], but we must honestly ask at what cost to the dignity and physical welfare of the local workers who are routinely treated as human pack animals. A mandatory, internationally enforceable code of ethics for guiding companies operating in these regions is long overdue.
texts.D: Focusing on the technical skill level of individual climbers is a convenient distraction from the larger and more fundamental issue: the sheer and entirely unsustainable volume of annual traffic on these mountains. Even the most technically accomplished and experienced climbers contribute meaningfully to the degradation of the mountain environment when they are forced to wait in dangerous high-altitude 'traffic jams' at 8,000 meters, burning precious resources and accumulating waste. [38]I share the firm view that the cumulative environmental impact of this industry is a disgrace demanding immediate collective action[/38], but I disagree that additional government regulation represents the most effective long-term answer. [40]Regional governments are simply too financially dependent on lucrative permit fees to ever meaningfully enforce strict visitor limits.[/40] The only genuinely lasting change will ultimately come from a deep and authentic cultural shift within the climbing community itself toward rigorous 'leave no trace' ethics and genuine environmental responsibility.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on who should be allowed to climb major peaks?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes adventure should be 'accessible to anyone' with funds. Expert A insists permits should be strictly limited to those with 'technical climbing experience.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the environmental impact of modern climbing?
questions.explanation: Expert A highlights the 'tons of oxygen canisters and human waste.' Expert D also describes the environmental impact as a 'disgrace' and a major issue.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the economic impact on local communities?
questions.explanation: Expert C acknowledges that the 'local economy benefits.' Expert B also states that commercialization has brought 'vital infrastructure and income' to these regions.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the effectiveness of government regulation?
questions.explanation: Expert D believes governments won't enforce limits because they depend on fees. Expert A, however, actively calls for the enforcement of 'strictly limited' permits, implying it is a viable path.
